meta.title: Backup Alarm
meta.description: Create an alarm that sounds a buzzer and flashes red LEDs when an object is within 30cm.
meta.learning_goal: Using ultrasonic sensors to trigger outputs based on distance detection
meta.code_hint: Read HC_SR04 distance, activate buzzer and LEDs when distance < 30cm

meta.title: Backup Alarm
meta.description: Create an alarm that sounds a buzzer and flashes red LEDs when an object is within 30cm.
meta.learning_goal: Using ultrasonic sensors to trigger outputs based on distance detection
meta.code_hint: Read HC_SR04 distance, activate buzzer and LEDs when distance < 30cm

walkthrough.instruction: Plug the HC-SR04 sensor into the breadboard with all four pins in column J: VCC in row 12, TRIG in row 11, ECHO in row 10, GND in row 9.
walkthrough.tip: The two silver circles are the ultrasonic 'eyes' — point them toward your target!

walkthrough.instruction: Place the buzzer with the long leg (+) in row 13, column E and the short leg (−) in row 16, column E.
walkthrough.tip: The long leg is positive — mark it with a tiny + on the buzzer.

walkthrough.instruction: Put the red LED on the breadboard with the long leg (anode) in row 22, column E and the short leg (cathode) in row 21, column E.
walkthrough.tip: The long leg is positive — it's called the anode!

walkthrough.instruction: Put the 220Ω resistor on the breadboard with one leg in row 21, column D and the other leg in row 17, column D.
walkthrough.tip: The resistor limits current so the component doesn't burn out.

walkthrough.instruction: Put the red LED on the breadboard with the long leg (anode) in row 28, column E and the short leg (cathode) in row 27, column E.
walkthrough.tip: The long leg is positive — it's called the anode!

walkthrough.instruction: Put the 220Ω resistor on the breadboard with one leg in row 27, column D and the other leg in row 23, column D.
walkthrough.tip: The resistor limits current so the component doesn't burn out.

walkthrough.instruction: Put the red LED on the breadboard with the long leg (anode) in row 22, column F and the short leg (cathode) in row 21, column F.
walkthrough.tip: The long leg is positive — it's called the anode!

walkthrough.instruction: Put the 220Ω resistor on the breadboard with one leg in row 21, column G and the other leg in row 17, column G.
walkthrough.tip: The resistor limits current so the component doesn't burn out.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a red wire from the Arduino's 5V pin to the breadboard at row 12, column I.
walkthrough.tip: This wire carries the signal between Arduino and your component.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a black wire from the Arduino's GND pin to the breadboard at row 9, column I.
walkthrough.tip: Black wires carry ground — the return path for electricity.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a green wire from Arduino pin D9 to the breadboard at row 11, column F.
walkthrough.tip: This wire carries the signal between Arduino and your component.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a green wire from the breadboard at row 10, column F to Arduino pin D10.
walkthrough.tip: This wire carries the signal between Arduino and your component.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a green wire from Arduino pin D3 to the breadboard at row 13, column A.
walkthrough.tip: This wire carries the signal between Arduino and your component.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a black wire from the breadboard at row 16, column D to the Arduino's GND pin.
walkthrough.tip: Black wires carry ground — the return path for electricity.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a green wire from Arduino pin D4 to the breadboard at row 22, column A.
walkthrough.tip: This wire carries the signal between Arduino and your component.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a black wire from the breadboard at row 17, column E to the Arduino's GND pin.
walkthrough.tip: Black wires carry ground — the return path for electricity.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a green wire from Arduino pin D5 to the breadboard at row 28, column A.
walkthrough.tip: This wire carries the signal between Arduino and your component.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a black wire from the breadboard at row 23, column E to the Arduino's GND pin.
walkthrough.tip: Black wires carry ground — the return path for electricity.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a green wire from Arduino pin D6 to the breadboard at row 22, column J.
walkthrough.tip: This wire carries the signal between Arduino and your component.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a black wire from the breadboard at row 17, column F to the Arduino's GND pin.
walkthrough.tip: Black wires carry ground — the return path for electricity.
